title: random signal: killed when building
body:
### Issue Description

I am randomly getting `signal: killed` from `podman build ` command ( or via API endpoint ), there is not any host resource pressure, dmesg and journald does not print anything useful ( there isnt any OOM info ).  Tried with btrfs and overlay driver.

Any idea how to investigate? 

### Steps to reproduce the issue

Steps to reproduce the issue
1. `podman -r build --no-cache --userns=auto -m 4096000000 --memory-swap -1 --cpu-shares 4096 -t foo .`



### Describe the results you received


comments.body:
Thanks for reaching out, @lukasmrtvy.  The memory settings look suspicious, so I _guess_ that `podman build` may be killed by the OOM killer.

It probably requires some manual investigation.  I usually `dnf install bcc-tools` and run `/usr/share/bcc/tools/killsnoop` to see investigate such signal issues.  The `TPID` in the output table is the _target_, so that is the process ID of the podman-build and/or the build containers.  You may need to run `execsnoop` in another terminal to lookup and cross-reference the IDs.

Thanks. 

Few notes to add:
- it does not happen on Docker on the same instance with the same workloads
- unfortunately  `--oom-kill-disable`  arg is not supported for builds to test the oom kill theory
- `node_vmstat_oom_kill` metric by node-exporter ( recommended by some K8s article, so I guess this would show potential OOMs for Podman ephemeral containers too ) shows always 0
 

Removed:
- --userns
-  -m  
- --memory-swap  
- -cpu-shares 

from API call and still getting `Signal: killed` randomly  ( host has enough free memory )
Have you tried it in rootful mode?

I had a similar problem and realized the underlying podman machine was only configured to 2048 despite my box having lots more. 

For me this fixed it. doubled the memory from 2048

```
podman machine stop podman-machine-default
podman machine set -m 4096 podman-machine-default
podman machine start podman-machine-default
```

@rhatdan yes, even in rootful mode

@chriskuchin thanks, but I am not using podman-machine

@rhatdan @flouthoc I guess this is not correct ( -m arg is not respected in rootless ) behavior for rootless
```
[root@ip-10-1-36-99 tmp]# cat << EOF > Dockerfile
FROM alpine
RUN apk add nginx
EOF
```

Are you running on cgroup V1?

No, [its ](https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/17918#issuecomment-1493407573) not happening anymore, probably some misconfiguration, sorry :( Anyway, I tried ebpf_exporter and there are no OOM kills happening on the instance. 

.. ,but seems that for some images I am getting a lot of similar errors like: ( usually these killed by signal: kill, containing nodejs )

Thanks, @lukasmrtvy!

Let's keep this issue open a bit longer just to make sure it doesn't occur anymore.

@vrothberg problem is still there, https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/17918#issuecomment-1493407573 comment was my misconfiguration, thats why its hidden now. Question is still if its related to https://github.com/systemd/systemd/issues/27028 , happens mostly for builds and with this https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/18031 might be related too.

Seems that `signal: Killed` is invoked from Podman API, it does happen if I use `podman -r build` , `podman build` is ok, it has to be some race condition, unfortunately, a lot of is happening on API, so I do not have a reproducer yet. Maybe streaming stats, or attaching can cause trouble to connections/streams from build API endpoint

Here is reproducer ( root user creating containers via sleeper`s rootless socket with enabled lingering, its happening even without ssh session,.. to our systemd service, this is just a reproducer  ):

```
# Fedora CoreOS 37.20230322.3.0 ignition:
variant: fcos
version: 1.4.0
passwd:
  users:
    - name: sleeper
      uid: 1001
storage:
  links:
    - path: /home/sleeper/.config/systemd/user/sockets.target.wants/podman.socket
      user:
        name: sleeper
      group:
        name: sleeper
      target: /usr/lib/systemd/user/podman.socket
      hard: false
  directories:
    - path: /home/sleeper/.config
      mode: 0755

        name: sleeper
      group:
        name: sleeper
    - path: /home/sleeper/.config/systemd
      mode: 0755

    - path: /home/sleeper/.config/systemd/user/sockets.target.wants
      mode: 0755
      user:
        name: sleeper
      group:
        name: sleeper
  files:
    - path: /var/lib/systemd/linger/sleeper
      mode: 0644
    - path: /tmp/Dockerfile
      contents:
        inline: |
          FROM busybox
          RUN sleep 60
```
as **root** user:
```
# spawn couple containers
podman -r --url unix:/run/user/1001/podman/podman.sock run -d docker.io/library/nginx
# this is important
podman -r --url unix:/run/user/1001/podman/podman.sock stats
```
as **root** user in another terminal:
```
# wait for Error: building at STEP "RUN sleep 60": while running runtime: signal: killed
cd /tmp
podman -r --url unix:/run/user/1001/podman/podman.sock build --no-cache . > /dev/null
Error: building at STEP "RUN sleep 60": while running runtime: signal: killed
```

```
# this is important
podman -r --url unix:/run/user/1001/podman/podman.sock stats
```

The `podman stats` part is puzzling me.  FWIW, I cannot reproduce.

@giuseppe @nalind have you seen something similar in the past?

@lukasmrtvy can you share the contents of the Dockerfile?

Update: it's in the ignition file :)

```
        name: sleeper
      group:
        name: sleeper
    - path: /home/sleeper/.config/systemd/user/sockets.target.wants
      mode: 0755
      user:
        name: sleeper
      group:
        name: sleeper
  files:
    - path: /var/lib/systemd/linger/sleeper
      mode: 0644
systemd:
  units:
    - name: wait.service
      enabled: true
      contents: |
        [Unit]
        Wants=network.target
        After=network-online.target

        [Service]
        Type=oneshot
        ExecStart=/bin/bash -c 'while true; do podman -r --url unix:/run/user/1001/podman/podman.sock info && break; done'

        [Install]
        WantedBy=multi-user.target
    - name: run.service
      enabled: true
      contents: |
        [Unit]
        Wants=network.target wait.service
        After=network-online.target wait.service

        [Service]
        Type=oneshot
        ExecStart=/bin/bash -c 'seq 20 | xargs -Iz podman -r --url unix:/run/user/1001/podman/podman.sock run -d docker.io/library/nginx'

        [Install]
        WantedBy=multi-user.target
    - name: stats.service
      enabled: true
      contents: |
        [Unit]
        Wants=network.target wait.service
        After=network-online.target wait.service run.service

        [Service]
        ExecStart=/bin/podman -r --url unix:/run/user/1001/podman/podman.sock stats

        [Install]
        WantedBy=multi-user.target
```

```
ssh core@<server>
sudo -s 
```

```
id
>>> uid=0(root) gid=0(root) groups=0(root) context=unconfined_u:unconfined_r:unconfined_t:s0-s0:c0.c1023
```

# build success
```
printf "%s\n" "FROM busybox" "RUN sleep 60"  > Dockerfile
tar -czf context.tar.gz Dockerfile
curl --unix-socket /run/user/1001/podman/podman.sock -X POST -H "Content-Type:application/tar"  --data-binary "@context.tar.gz"  "http://d/v4.4.4/libpod/build?nocache=true"
```

# build failed
```
printf "%s\n" "FROM busybox" "RUN sleep 60"  > /home/sleeper/Dockerfile
podman  -r --url unix:/run/user/1001/podman/podman.sock build -f /home/sleeper/Dockerfile --no-cache .
>>> Error: building at STEP "RUN sleep 60": while running runtime: signal: killed
```

@vrothberg maybe its caused by client? buffering? stream? ideas?

Running `killsnoop` on the AWS instance did not give me much data on who's killing what:
```
[...]
12:40:58  12311  crun             0    11981  0 
12:40:58  12312  crun             0    11981  0 
12:40:58  12313  crun             0    11981  0 
12:40:58  12314  crun             0    11981  0 
12:40:58  12315  crun             0    11981  0 
12:40:58  12316  crun             0    11981  0 
12:40:58  12317  crun             0    11981  0 
12:40:58  12320  crun             0    11981  0 
12:40:58  12321  crun             0    11981  0 
12:40:58  12322  crun             0    11981  0 
12:40:59  12323  crun             0    11981  0 
12:40:59  1088   podman           9    11982  0 
```

`crun` is waiting for the build container to finish.  The `9` comes from podman and is sent to slirp4netns.

@giuseppe, do you have a suspicion why it only happens when `stats` and `build` are run remotely and in parallel?

It is caused by `prctl(PR_SET_PDEATHSIG)` that is not safe to use in a Go program (unless we lock the thread).

PR here: https://github.com/containers/buildah/pull/4790
